name: Soria Highlands Loop
name_original: Pueblos de las Tierras Altas — circular por Santa Cruz de Yanguas, San Pedro Manrique, Villar de Maya y Oncala
description: This difficult 53.1-mile road cycling route through the Soria Highlands offers historic villages and sweeping views.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Enciso traverse a diverse landscape in La Rioja, characterized by a transition from fertile plains to rugged mountains. The region features the Cidacos River Valley, offering mostly paved surfaces ideal for road bikes, alongside rolling hills and significant mountain passes in the Cameros and Cantabria Ranges. This varied terrain provides options for both moderate rides and challenging climbs, often on solitary tarmacs through natural settings. Best road cycling routes around Enciso The most popular road cycling route is Valle del Rio Cidacos and ancient town of Turruncun — circular from Arnedillo, a 40.8 miles (65.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 19 minutes to complete. This difficult route follows the Cidacos River Valley, showcasing a transition from fertile plains to more mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Soria Highlands Loop, a difficult 53.1 miles (85.5 km) path. This route explores highland areas, offering varied terrain and elevation gains. Local road cyclists also love the Sierra de Alcarama – Cornago Loop, a 76.9 miles (123.7 km) trail leading through the Sierra de Alcarama, often completed in about 6 hours 12 minutes. Road cycling around Enciso is defined by the Cidacos River Valley, rolling hills, and mountain passes. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Enciso are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 130 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Enciso's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Enciso?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes available around Enciso, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for road cycling routes in Enciso?
A: Enciso's road cycling routes offer a mix of difficulty levels. You'll find options ranging from easy routes, often following the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1814079" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cidacos Greenway&lt;/a&gt;, to more challenging paths with significant elevation gains through the rolling hills and mountain passes of the Cameros and Cantabria Ranges. The region has 1 easy route, 9 moderate routes, and 6 difficult routes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Enciso?
A: The terrain around Enciso is quite varied. You can expect routes that traverse the fertile plains of the Cidacos River Valley, offering mostly paved surfaces. As you venture further, you'll encounter rolling hills and significant mountain passes, providing challenging climbs and rewarding descents, often on solitary tarmacs through stunning natural settings.

Q: What interesting landmarks or attractions can I see along road cycling routes near Enciso?
A: Many routes offer opportunities to explore the region's rich history and natural beauty. You can cycle past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5344963" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Préjano Castle&lt;/a&gt;, visit the historic village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2718792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Yanguas&lt;/a&gt;, or even encounter the famous Dinosaur Route with its well-preserved footprints and educational panels. The ruins of the medieval Castle of Enciso also provide breathtaking panoramic views.

Q: Can I combine road cycling with exploring the region's history, like the Dinosaur Route?
A: Absolutely. Enciso is renowned for its Dinosaur Route, featuring over 1,400 dinosaur footprints. Cycling to and between paleontological sites like Valdecevillo, La Senoba, and Virgen del Campo offers a unique blend of outdoor activity and historical exploration. The Paleontological Center of Enciso provides further insights into the region's ancient past.

Q: Are there opportunities to visit vineyards or wineries while road cycling in La Rioja?
A: Yes, the wider La Rioja region is famous for its wine. Many routes will take you through sprawling vineyards, offering an authentic taste of Spain's winemaking heritage. While specific routes may not directly lead to winery tours, the landscape is dominated by vineyards, and you'll find opportunities to explore this aspect of the region during your cycling trips.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Enciso?
A: Yes, there are routes suitable for beginners and families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1814079" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cidacos Greenway&lt;/a&gt;, a former railway line, is an excellent option. It's mostly paved and offers a moderate and enjoyable ride through attractive scenery, making it ideal for those seeking a less challenging experience.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Enciso?
A: The best time for road cycling in Enciso, La Rioja, is generally during the spring and autumn months. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather, making for more comfortable rides. The region's diverse landscapes, from fertile plains to rugged mountains, are particularly beautiful during these times.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Enciso?
A: Yes, Enciso offers several circular road cycling routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990105641/valle-del-rio-cidacos-and-ancient-town-of-turruncun-circular-from-arnedillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle del Rio Cidacos and ancient town of Turruncun — circular from Arnedillo&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990927883/highlands-villages-circular-through-santa-cruz-de-yanguas-san-pedro-manrique-villar-de-maya-and-oncala" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Highlands Villages - circular through Santa Cruz de Yanguas, San Pedro Manrique, Villar de Maya and Oncala&lt;/a&gt;, which explores the highland villages.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Enciso?
A: While specific parking areas for each trailhead are not detailed, Enciso and surrounding towns are generally accommodating. Look for public parking in the villages where routes typically start or pass through. It's advisable to check local regulations or signs upon arrival.

Q: Is public transport available to access road cycling routes in Enciso?
A: Public transport options to directly access specific road cycling trailheads in Enciso might be limited. It's generally recommended to travel by car to reach the starting points of many routes. However, some routes, particularly those along the Cidacos River Valley, may be accessible from larger towns with better public transport connections.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in the Enciso region pass through charming villages and towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and local shops for refreshments and food. The region's focus on cycling tourism means that amenities for cyclists are often available, especially in more populated areas or along popular routes.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Enciso?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 120 road cyclists. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, from the scenic Cidacos River Valley to the challenging mountain passes, and the opportunity to cycle on solitary tarmacs through beautiful natural settings and vineyards.
tips:
Santa Cruz de Yanguas is a municipality in Soria in the Tierras Altas region, community of Castilla y León. During the Middle Ages it was part of the Community of Villa y Tierra de Yanguas.

It is located in the Baos River basin, and has important forest and mycological resources. The municipal area is part of the Urbión Regional Reserve, with a large presence of deer, roe deer, wild boar and woodcock.

Of its heritage, the Roman bridge that spans the bed of the Baos River, the church of the Santísima Trinidad (16th century) in Gothic style and the hermitage of the Virgen del Pilar stand out. As in the rest of the region, you can find dinosaur footprints (ichnites).

Population that is located to the south of the Sierra de San Cristrobal and that is crossed by the Río Linaris. The town, in the middle of the Iberian System, is located in the place called Tierras Altas.

Located in a fantastic setting, today crossed by a road, this beautiful Gothic church, from the 16th century, stands out for the altarpieces it contains. The location of the church is at the entrance of the valley, after the Sierra de San Cristóbal.
Enciso is in a mountainous area of the Iberian System, on the border with Soria. The Cidacos River crosses it and is known for having 3000 dinosaur footprints.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This racebike route, covering approximately 85.5 km with over 1300 meters of elevation gain, is classified as difficult. Komoot estimates the average cycling time to be around 4 hours and 14 minutes. However, this can vary significantly based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and weather conditions.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as difficult. The Soria Highlands are characterized by hilly terrain with significant climbs and descents. Expect demanding sections and many curves, especially on roads leading to passes like Oncala. While primarily paved for racebikes, the elevation changes require good fitness and experience.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Highlands Villages cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the villages along the route. Starting points like Santa Cruz de Yanguas or San Pedro Manrique are good options, offering village amenities and public parking areas. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival for specific parking regulations.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers picturesque scenery through the Soria Highlands. You'll cycle through a diverse landscape of mountains, valleys, forests, and traditional Spanish countryside. Expect sweeping views, charming historic villages, olive groves, and quiet roads, providing a serene cycling experience away from heavy traffic.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during the spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October). During these seasons, the weather is typically milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially snowy conditions of winter in the highlands.

faqs.question: Are there any notable points of interest or villages along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through several charming villages and points of interest. You'll cycle through Santa Cruz de Yanguas, San Pedro Manrique, Villar de Maya, and Oncala. Highlights include the Church of Santa María and the historic architecture of these traditional settlements.

faqs.question: What is the road surface like on this route?
faqs.answer: As a racebike tour, the route is predominantly on paved roads. However, given the rural nature of the Soria Highlands, some sections might have varying surface quality. While generally suitable for road bikes, be prepared for potential rougher patches or older asphalt in certain areas between villages.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants available on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you will find opportunities for refreshments and meals in the villages along the route. San Pedro Manrique and Oncala are good places to look for local restaurants and cafes to refuel during your ride.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this area?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. The area is part of the Reserva de la Biosfera Valles del Leza, Jubera, Cidacos y Alhama, which focuses on conservation and sustainable development, but does not impose entry fees for general access.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, significant elevation gain (over 1300m), and 85.5 km distance, this route is not recommended for beginners or young families. It requires a good level of fitness and cycling experience. More gentle, shorter routes would be better suited for family outings in the Soria region.

faqs.question: Is the Highlands Villages cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in the natural areas of the Soria Highlands, this specific route is designed for racebiking on public roads. If you plan to bring a dog, it would need to be able to keep pace safely alongside your bike or be transported in a suitable trailer, which might not be practical for a difficult racebike tour.
